subsystem: sudo/sudoers policy plugin — sudoers_policy_deserialize_info(), the function that deserializes the sudo front-end's plugin_args, settings[] and user_info[] key=value arrays into the sudoers plugin's global execution state (identity, mode flags, paths, host/tty, timeouts, etc.).
provenance: Identified by exact symbol and library idioms: the function name sudoers_policy_deserialize_info, the struct sudoers_open_info, debug_decl/debug_return_int/SUDOERS_DEBUG_PLUGIN, the U_() gettext wrapper, sudo_warn/sudo_warnx, sudo_strtoid/sudo_strtonum/sudo_strtomode/sudo_strtobool, set_interfaces, sudo_parse_gids, and the MODE_* flag family are all part of the sudo/sudoers source tree (plugins/sudoers/policy.c). The sample header comment 'primevul_c_validation_0214_fixed' and the filename train_0048_cwe193.c indicate a PrimeVul-derived sample tagged for CWE-193 (off-by-one).
trust_boundary: The single argument void *v is cast to struct sudoers_open_info *info; its three members plugin_args, settings and user_info are NULL-terminated arrays of C strings supplied by the sudo front-end across the policy plugin API. The front-end is nominally trusted (setuid root), but the whole point of this deserializer is to defensively validate what the front-end sends — the in-code warnings 'set by sudo front-end' and 'invalid mode flags from sudo front end' show the authors treat these strings as potentially malformed. Every *cur string, and in particular the value bytes after each 'key=' prefix, is the hostile input surface. Values flow into privilege-relevant globals (sudoers_uid/gid/mode, sudoers_file, runas_user/group, login_class, path_ldap_secret, mode flags) so incorrect parsing has security weight even though no fixed-size stack buffer is filled here.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point of the deserializer. The opaque void *v is reinterpreted as struct sudoers_open_info *info, which carries the three input string arrays. edit_mask is the whitelist of mode bits permitted when sudoedit is requested (MODE_EDIT|MODE_IGNORE_TICKET|MODE_NONINTERACTIVE) and is used later as a security gate. Locals p/errstr are scratch parse cursors, groups/remhost hold deferred values, cur is the iterator over each string array, and flags accumulates the MODE_* bits the front-end asked for and is the function's return value. debug_decl sets up the sudo debug subsystem scope.

MATCHES(s, v) is the prefix test used for every option. Because v is always a string LITERAL, sizeof(v) is the array size including the NUL, so sizeof(v)-1 is exactly strlen(v). strncmp therefore compares the first strlen(v) bytes of the candidate string s against the literal key (which always ends in '='). A match means s begins with 'key=', and crucially guarantees s is at least strlen(v) bytes long, so indexing s[strlen(v)] afterward is in-bounds.

INVALID(v) prints a warning naming the offending key, using sizeof(v)-2 as the length (strlen(v) minus the trailing '=') so the '=' is not shown. CHECK(s, v) enforces that an option that requires a non-empty value actually has one: it inspects the byte at index sizeof(v)-1 — i.e. the first byte AFTER the matched 'key=' prefix — and if that byte is the string terminator '\0' the value is empty, so it warns and jumps to bad. The correctness of CHECK depends entirely on the literal v passed to CHECK being the SAME literal that was passed to the preceding MATCHES, because sizeof(v)-1 must equal the length of the matched prefix for it to index the true value-start byte.

Before parsing, the code records the submission wall-clock time into sudo_user.submit_time (used later for time-based sudoers rules and ticket freshness). If the clock read fails it aborts to bad. No untrusted data involved.

First parse loop, over sudo.conf plugin args. It iterates until the NULL sentinel. For error_recovery= it advances the pointer past the prefix (*cur + strlen("error_recovery=")) and parses a boolean; an unparseable value is a non-fatal warning (the global sudoers_recovery is simply left unchanged), otherwise sudoers_recovery is set. The value pointer arithmetic relies on the NUL-termination guaranteed by MATCHES.

The remaining plugin_args set privilege-critical globals: sudoers_file (path to the sudoers policy file, CHECK'd non-empty), sudoers_uid and sudoers_gid (owner ids the sudoers file must have, parsed with sudo_strtoid and rejected on errstr), sudoers_mode (required file permissions, parsed with sudo_strtomode), and path_ldap_conf / path_ldap_secret (LDAP config/secret paths, CHECK'd non-empty). For the string-path options the code stores an interior pointer into the front-end's own buffer rather than copying. These values govern which file sudo trusts as its ruleset and what ownership/mode it demands, so they are the most security-sensitive settings, though they are supposed to originate from sudo.conf (root-controlled).

Second loop, over the command-line-derived settings[] array. closefrom= is bounded to [3, INT_MAX] via sudo_strtonum, which returns 0 on any range/format error (then errstr is set and the code aborts). This is the general pattern for numeric settings: strtonum with explicit min/max and a 0-return error convention, which is the safe way to parse untrusted integers.

String settings: cmnd_chroot/cmnd_cwd (chroot/cwd for the command), runas_user/runas_group (target identity, which also SET a RUNAS_*_SPECIFIED flag on sudo_user.flags), and prompt (deliberately allowed to be empty, so it has NO CHECK). Each stores an interior pointer into the front-end string. runas_user/runas_group directly control which account the command runs as, so their integrity matters, but they are validated later by the sudoers ruleset.

The boolean mode options (set_home, preserve_environment, run_shell, login_shell, implied_shell, preserve_groups, ignore_ticket, noninteractive, sudoedit) are all routed through parse_bool(line, len, &flags, mode_bit). Note the length argument is sizeof("keyword") - 1 WITHOUT the trailing '=' — e.g. sizeof("set_home")-1 == 8 for the key "set_home=" whose prefix length is 9. This means parse_bool receives the length of the keyword excluding '=', and its implementation (not shown) must internally skip len+1 bytes (past the '=') to reach the value; the +1 is an implicit contract. On a parse failure parse_bool returns -1 and the caller aborts; otherwise it OR/ANDs the given MODE_* bit into flags.

Platform-conditional string settings: login_class (BSD login.conf class), Solaris privilege sets runas_privs/runas_limitprivs under HAVE_PRIV_SET, SELinux user_role/user_type under HAVE_SELINUX, and BSD-auth login_style under HAVE_BSD_AUTH_H. Each is CHECK'd non-empty and then pointed into the front-end buffer. login_class also flips def_use_loginclass true. These are pointers, not copies, so the front-end string must outlive the plugin's use of them (a lifetime contract the caller owns).

The BSD-auth branch matches the prefix "bsdauth_type=" (length 13) but performs its emptiness CHECK against the DIFFERENT literal "login_style=" (length 12). Because CHECK indexes s[sizeof(v)-1], it tests byte 12 of the string, whereas the actual value begins at byte 13 (sizeof("bsdauth_type=")-1). Byte 12 is the '=' that MATCHES already confirmed present, so it is never '\0' and the empty-value guard can never fire here — see the corresponding what_could_go_wrong step.

network_addrs= is handed to set_interfaces() (which itself parses a list of ip/mask pairs — a secondary untrusted parser); max_groups= is bounded [1, INT_MAX]; remote_host= is CHECK'd and stashed in remhost for later; timeout= is parsed by parse_timeout with ERANGE handling. Note max_groups later drives group-array sizing elsewhere, so its bound matters. These end the settings loop.

The sudoedit privilege gate: if MODE_EDIT is set, flags may contain ONLY the bits in edit_mask; any extra bit means the front-end tried to combine sudoedit with a disallowed mode and the request is rejected. This is the key security validation the function performs. Then user_gid/sid/uid/umask are pre-seeded to the -1 sentinel so the later 'must be specified' checks can detect whether user_info supplied them. Note user_uid is cast to (gid_t)-1 rather than (uid_t)-1 — a cosmetic type mismatch (both yield all-ones).

Third loop, over user_info[]. Unlike settings, several of these are strdup'd (owned copies): user (user_name), cwd (user_cwd), tty (user_ttypath), host (user_host). uid/gid/sid are parsed with sudo_strtoid, umask with sudo_strtomode, lines/cols with strtonum bounds. The host handler additionally derives a short hostname user_shost: if a '.' is present it strndup's the leading label, otherwise it ALIASES user_shost = user_host (two pointers to one allocation). The tty handler strips a leading _PATH_DEV prefix by advancing the pointer.

Mandatory-field validation: user_name, user_uid, user_gid and user_host must all have been supplied, else the request is rejected — this prevents downstream code from operating on unset identity. user_runhost is then the remote host if remote_host= was given, else the local host, strdup'd into its own allocation; user_srunhost is derived the same short-name way as user_shost, again aliasing user_runhost when there is no dot.

Defaulting and finalization: user_cwd/user_runcwd/user_tty default to strdup("unknown")/the cwd if unset; the deferred groups string is expanded by sudo_parse_gids into user_gids/user_ngids; user_umask defaults to the process umask if the front-end did not send one; MODE_LOGIN_SHELL forces def_env_reset; cmnd_fd is initialized to -1; and the settings and user_info arrays are dumped to the debug log. On success debug_return_int(flags) returns the accumulated mode flags; the oom/bad labels return MODE_ERROR after a warning.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Off-by-one (CWE-193) in the emptiness guard: the branch is entered on prefix "bsdauth_type=" (13 chars) but CHECK is invoked with "login_style=" (12 chars). CHECK expands to `if ((*cur)[sizeof("login_style=") - 1] == '\0')`, i.e. it inspects byte index 12 — the '=' that MATCHES already verified — instead of byte index 13 where the value actually starts. Since byte 12 is never '\0', the guard can NEVER trigger, so a front-end sending exactly "bsdauth_type=" (empty value) is accepted and login_style is set to point at the empty string rather than being rejected as invalid. The impact is bounded (no out-of-bounds read/write occurs, because index 12 is inside the matched-and-NUL-terminated prefix, and an empty login_style is benign), so this is a low-severity correctness/off-by-one defect rather than a memory-safety hole — but it is the exact class the sample is tagged for and the CHECK literal should be "bsdauth_type=" to match the branch.

Latent off-by-one contract in parse_bool (implementation not shown). Every parse_bool call passes sizeof("keyword") - 1, the length of the keyword WITHOUT the '=' separator (8 for "set_home", whose matched prefix "set_home=" is 9 bytes). parse_bool must therefore internally advance by len + 1 to skip the '=' and reach the value; if the implementation used line + len (forgetting the +1) it would parse the literal '=' as the boolean value, and if it used line + len + strlen("=") inconsistently across keywords it could read the wrong byte. Because the caller and callee split the +1 responsibility, this is exactly where a CWE-193 could hide. The invariant to confirm: parse_bool skips len+1 bytes and never reads past the NUL for an empty value like "set_home=".

Pointer-aliasing lifetime contract (latent double-free / use-after-free, CWE-415/CWE-416). When user_host contains no '.', user_shost is made an ALIAS of user_host (one heap allocation, two globals); the same pattern aliases user_srunhost to user_runhost at lines 393-399. The teardown code (not shown) MUST free these conditionally — freeing both user_host and user_shost when they alias is a double-free, and freeing one then using the other is a use-after-free. This is safe here only if the cleanup path checks `user_shost != user_host` before freeing. Worth confirming in policy.c's cleanup routine.

Trust / missing-independent-validation surface (CWE-20). Privilege-defining globals — sudoers_file (which file sudo reads its ruleset from), sudoers_uid/sudoers_gid/sudoers_mode (the ownership and permissions sudo will DEMAND of that file), path_ldap_secret, and runas_user/runas_group — are taken verbatim from the front-end plugin_args/settings with only format/emptiness checks. If an attacker could influence these plugin args (e.g. a compromised or spoofed front-end, or a mis-parsed sudo.conf), pointing sudoers_file at an attacker-controlled file or loosening sudoers_uid/mode expectations would let a malicious ruleset be trusted. The invariant is that plugin_args originate only from root-owned sudo.conf and settings from the trusted front-end; this function does not (and cannot) re-authenticate them, so the security rests on the caller's provenance guarantees.

Delegated secondary parser (latent, CWE-20/CWE-125). network_addrs= is passed uncopied into set_interfaces(), which itself tokenizes a front-end-supplied list of address/mask pairs — a separate untrusted parser not shown here. Any bounds error in that address/mask tokenizer (over-long tokens, malformed masks) would be reachable through this line. This branch has no CHECK for emptiness, but set_interfaces returning false aborts, so the risk lives entirely inside set_interfaces; confirm it bounds each token against its destination.

Type-confusion smell (CWE-704, informational). user_uid is initialized with a (gid_t) cast instead of (uid_t), while the matching guard at line 378 compares against (uid_t)-1. On every real platform uid_t and gid_t are the same width so both produce the all-ones sentinel and the comparison still works, but the inconsistent cast is fragile: on a hypothetical platform where gid_t is narrower than uid_t the sentinel could differ and the 'user-ID not set' check could misfire. No exploit, but it should read (uid_t)-1.

summary: This is sudo's sudoers_policy_deserialize_info(), a defensive key=value deserializer for front-end-supplied plugin args, settings and user_info. It is a well-structured parser that stores interior pointers or strdup'd copies and validates numbers with bounded strtonum — no fixed-size buffer is filled, so there is no classic overflow here. The only concrete defect in the shown code is a genuine off-by-one (CWE-193): the BSD-auth branch CHECKs the wrong literal ("login_style=" vs "bsdauth_type="), so its empty-value guard is dead — a low-severity correctness bug, not a memory-safety one. The main latent contracts to verify downstream are parse_bool's len+1 value-offset arithmetic and the user_shost/user_srunhost pointer aliasing (double-free hazard).

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry signature; opaque void* cast to the input struct — reviewed as trust boundary, no bug in the cast itself.
Whitelist constant used by the sudoedit gate at line 266; correctly restricts flags, no issue.
Container of the three untrusted string arrays; establishes the trust boundary, not itself a defect.
Uses sizeof(literal)-1 == strlen; guarantees the candidate is at least prefix-length long, so subsequent indexing is in-bounds. Safe.
Formats a warning with sizeof(v)-2 length; bounded by the literal, no user-controlled format string. Safe.
Correctness depends on the v literal equaling the MATCHES literal; violated at the bsdauth_type branch (line 216) — see off-by-one finding.
No untrusted input; failure is handled by goto bad.
sudo_strtobool on the value; invalid is non-fatal, global left unchanged. Safe.
Privilege-relevant path taken from front-end; part of the CWE-20 trust finding.
Owner-id expectations from front-end, parsed with sudo_strtoid; part of the CWE-20 trust finding.
sudo_strtomode with errstr check; parsed safely, folded into the trust discussion.
CHECK'd non-empty interior pointers; part of the trust surface, no parsing bug.
sudo_strtonum bounded [3, INT_MAX] with 0-error convention. Safe.
CHECK'd interior pointers; no copy, no bound to exceed.
CHECK'd, set identity pointers plus RUNAS_*_SPECIFIED flags; validated later by sudoers rules.
Intentionally allows empty value (no CHECK by design); just an interior pointer. Safe.
Length arg is sizeof(keyword)-1 excluding '='; parse_bool must skip len+1 — latent off-by-one contract (CWE-193).
CHECK'd interior pointer; sets def_use_loginclass. Safe.
CHECK'd interior pointers under platform ifdef; consistent literals. Safe.
CHECK'd interior pointers with matching literals. Safe.
CHECK literal differs from MATCHES literal — off-by-one index, empty-value guard is dead (CWE-193 finding).
Uncopied value handed to a secondary untrusted parser; latent delegated-parser risk.
strtonum bounded [1, INT_MAX]; drives later group sizing but is bounded here. Safe.
CHECK'd, deferred into remhost for user_runhost. Safe.
parse_timeout with ERANGE/invalid handling and abort. Safe.
CHECK'd interior pointer under ifdef; matching literal. Safe.
Correct security gate: rejects any flag outside edit_mask when MODE_EDIT is set. This is a defense, not a defect.
Sentinel initialized with a gid_t cast but compared as uid_t at line 378 — type-confusion smell (informational).
CHECK'd then strdup'd owned copies; OOM handled. tty _PATH_DEV strip advances within the copy. Safe.
sudo_strtoid/strtonum/strtomode with errstr checks and bounds. Safe.
user_shost aliases user_host when no dot — latent double-free/UAF lifetime contract for cleanup.
Rejects unset identity before use; a correct validation gate.
Same aliasing pattern as user_shost; folded into the pointer-aliasing lifetime finding.
strdup("unknown")/cwd defaults with OOM handling. Safe.
groups was CHECK'd non-empty; parse errors return -1 and abort. Delegated but guarded.
Falls back to process umask; no untrusted input. Safe.
sudo_debug_printf with a fixed format and the string as an argument (not the format). Safe.
Error epilogue returns MODE_ERROR; no resource logic that could double-free here. Safe.

findings.title: Off-by-one wrong-literal in bsdauth_type empty-value CHECK
findings.bug_class: Off-by-one / wrong-length guard (validation checks the wrong byte)
findings.invariant: The literal passed to CHECK must be identical to the literal passed to the matching MATCHES so that sizeof(v)-1 indexes the first byte AFTER the matched 'key=' prefix (byte 13 for "bsdauth_type="), which is where the value or its NUL lives.
findings.failure_mode: MATCHES uses "bsdauth_type=" (length 13) but CHECK uses "login_style=" (length 12), so CHECK tests byte 12 — the already-matched '=' — instead of byte 13. Byte 12 is never '\0', so the empty-value guard is dead code: a front-end that sends exactly "bsdauth_type=" gets login_style set to the empty string instead of being rejected with an 'invalid ... set by sudo front-end' warning. No out-of-bounds access occurs because byte 12 is inside the NUL-terminated matched prefix; the impact is limited to accepting an empty BSD-auth style. Fix: CHECK(*cur, "bsdauth_type=").
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the intended literal is "bsdauth_type="; verify downstream login_style handling tolerates (or the fixed CHECK now rejects) an empty style, and grep the other CHECK sites to ensure every CHECK literal equals its MATCHES literal.

findings.title: parse_bool receives keyword length excluding '=' — latent value-offset off-by-one
findings.invariant: parse_bool must interpret its len argument as the keyword length WITHOUT the '=' and advance to the value at line + len + 1 (skipping the '='), and it must stop safely at the NUL for an empty value such as "set_home=".
findings.failure_mode: Every boolean handler passes sizeof("keyword")-1 (no '='), e.g. 8 for the 9-byte prefix "set_home=". If parse_bool's unshown body advances by len instead of len+1 it parses the '=' character as the boolean; if it advances too far it reads past the value. Because the +1 responsibility is split between the constant here and code not shown, this is the natural home for a CWE-193; it is only safe if parse_bool does line+len+1 and treats an empty tail as an invalid boolean returning -1.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read parse_bool in policy.c: confirm it computes the value pointer as line + len + 1 and that sudo_strtobool on an empty string yields -1 so "keyword=" (empty) is rejected rather than misread.

findings.title: Short-hostname pointer aliasing creates a double-free / use-after-free lifetime contract
findings.bug_class: Pointer aliasing / dangerous ownership (double-free or use-after-free in cleanup)
findings.invariant: Any teardown that frees user_host/user_runhost must NOT also free user_shost/user_srunhost when they alias the same allocation; it must guard with a pointer-inequality check (e.g. if (user_shost != user_host) free(user_shost)).
findings.failure_mode: When the host name contains no '.', user_shost is set equal to user_host (line 329), and identically user_srunhost equals user_runhost (line 398). If the plugin's cleanup routine unconditionally frees both members it double-frees the single allocation; if it frees one and then dereferences the other it is a use-after-free. A front-end can trivially trigger the aliasing branch by sending a dotless host ("host=localhost"). This is a latent contract on the cleanup code, not a bug visible in this function.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect the sudoers policy cleanup path for user_host/user_shost and user_runhost/user_srunhost: verify each free is guarded by a pointer-equality test so aliased buffers are freed exactly once.

findings.title: Privilege-defining settings trusted verbatim from the front-end
findings.anchor: sudoers_file / sudoers_uid / sudoers_gid / sudoers_mode / path_ldap_secret assignments
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation / over-trust of caller-supplied configuration
findings.invariant: plugin_args/settings must originate only from the root-owned sudo.conf and the trusted setuid front-end; this deserializer performs format/emptiness checks but does NOT independently authenticate these paths and ids, so their integrity is guaranteed by provenance, not by this code.
findings.failure_mode: The globals that decide which file sudo trusts as its ruleset (sudoers_file) and the ownership/mode it demands of it (sudoers_uid/gid/mode), plus path_ldap_secret and runas identity, are copied straight from the front-end strings. If an attacker could inject these plugin args (compromised front-end, spoofed plugin invocation, or a mis-parsed sudo.conf), redirecting sudoers_file or relaxing the owner/mode expectations could cause a malicious ruleset to be honored — a privilege-escalation primitive. Within this function there is no bug; the risk is the strength of the caller's provenance guarantees.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the plugin loader restricts plugin_args to root-owned sudo.conf and that sudoers_uid/sudoers_mode are still enforced against the actual sudoers file's stat() before it is parsed, so a front-end-supplied path cannot bypass ownership checks.

findings.title: user_uid sentinel initialized with a gid_t cast
findings.bug_class: Incorrect type in expression (sentinel type mismatch)
findings.invariant: The 'unset' sentinel written to user_uid must be the same value the later guard compares against (user_uid == (uid_t)-1 at line 378), which requires the sentinel to be cast to uid_t.
findings.failure_mode: user_uid is seeded as (gid_t)-1 while the mandatory-field check at line 378 tests (uid_t)-1. On all mainstream platforms uid_t and gid_t are identically sized, so both are all-ones and the check works; but the mismatched cast is a latent portability hazard — on a platform where gid_t were narrower than uid_t the two sentinels could differ and the 'user-ID not set' guard could false-negative. Purely cosmetic today; should read (uid_t)-1.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify uid_t and gid_t widths match on all target platforms, or change the initializer to (uid_t)-1 to match the comparison at line 378.

audit_checklist: Fix and confirm every CHECK(s, v) uses the SAME literal as its preceding MATCHES(*cur, v); the bsdauth_type=/login_style= pair (line 216) is currently mismatched.
Read parse_bool(): confirm it advances to the value at line + len + 1 (the len argument excludes '='), and that an empty value like "keyword=" returns -1 rather than reading past the NUL.
In the sudoers policy cleanup path, verify user_shost/user_host and user_srunhost/user_runhost are freed with a pointer-inequality guard so the dotless-hostname aliasing cannot double-free (CWE-415).

Confirm plugin_args (sudoers_file, sudoers_uid/gid/mode, path_ldap_secret) come only from root-owned sudo.conf and that sudoers_uid/mode are still enforced against the real sudoers file's stat() before parsing.
Verify set_interfaces() safely bounds each token of the front-end network_addrs= list against its destinations.
Confirm the sudoedit gate (flags & edit_mask) != flags at line 266 covers every dangerous mode bit that must not co-occur with MODE_EDIT.
Change user_uid = (gid_t)-1 (line 273) to (uid_t)-1 to match the comparison at line 378, or confirm uid_t/gid_t are the same width on all targets.
Confirm strdup/strndup OOM paths (goto oom) leave no partially-initialized aliased globals that a later cleanup would double-free.
